title: The World’s Greatest Song That Simply Shouldn’t Exist: The Making of The Beatles’ “A Day in the Life”
description:
in History, Music |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Were we to take bets on the sin­gle most wide­ly heard alarm clock, the one includ
body:
in History, Music |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Were we to take bets on the sin­gle most wide­ly heard alarm clock, the one includ­ed on the Bea­t­les’ “A Day in the Life” would sure­ly be a pop­u­lar choice. It comes, you’ll recall, at the begin­ning of the song’s mid­dle sec­tion, when Paul takes over from John and sings about wak­ing up, comb­ing his hair, catch­ing a bus, and hav­ing a smoke. Giv­en the lyrics’ sub­ject mat­ter, its inclu­sion seems nat­ur­al, and even like one of the most straight­for­ward­ly explic­a­ble choic­es in the entire record­ing. In fact, the alarm clock was nev­er meant to stay in the mix: the band’s assis­tant Mal Evans set it off as a gag after doing a place­hold­er count of 24 mea­sures in the mid­dle of the ses­sions, but it just hap­pened to work per­fect­ly with the form the song even­tu­al­ly took.
That’s just one of the for­tu­itous acci­dents music YouTu­ber David Hart­ley recounts in the video above, on the com­plete mak­ing of “A Day in the Life.” As most Bea­t­les fans know, John real­ly did take his ini­tial inspi­ra­tion from the news­pa­per, and in par­tic­u­lar a cou­ple of arti­cles about a Guin­ness heir’s fatal car crash and 4,000 holes in Black­burn, Lan­cashire.
But after he wrote his vers­es, he found him­self at a loss as to how to devel­op the song from there. Paul offered the bit he’d already writ­ten about falling out of bed and drag­ging a comb across his head. It was­n’t long before the Bea­t­les began record­ing — and doing so for the first time, Hart­ley notes, before hav­ing fin­ished writ­ing the song. They had dis­parate pieces of music to join togeth­er, but no idea, yet, of how they’d do it.
Hence those 24 emp­ty bars. Play­ing a series of ear­ly takes, Hart­ley shows how they got to be filled in, draw­ing heav­i­ly on Here, There, and Every­where: My Life Record­ing the Music of the Bea­t­les, the mem­oir of engi­neer Geoff Emer­ick. John’s idea of “a sound that began real­ly small, and then grad­u­al­ly expand­ed into some­thing huge” inspired Paul’s sug­ges­tion of a sym­pho­ny orches­tra, in response to which pro­duc­er George Mar­tin expressed con­cerns about the cost of hir­ing one. Ringo, in turn, jok­ing­ly pro­posed hir­ing half the orches­tra and hav­ing them play the part twice, which turned out to be a prag­mat­ic solu­tion. As for how to end a song like this, the use of “maybe the most famous sin­gle chord in record­ed his­to­ry” — fol­lowed by “a high-fre­quen­cy tone only dogs could hear and a short loop of non­sense sound” — came as the result of a process we can only call Beat­lesque.
Relat­ed Con­tent:
How John Lennon Wrote the Bea­t­les’ Best Song, “A Day in the Life”
Based in Seoul, Col­in Marshall writes and broad­casts on cities, lan­guage, and cul­ture. He’s the author of the newslet­ter Books on Cities as well as the books 한국 요약 금지 (No Sum­ma­riz­ing Korea) and Kore­an Newtro. Fol­low him on the social net­work for­mer­ly known as Twit­ter at @colinmarshall.

schema.headline: The World’s Greatest Song That Simply Shouldn’t Exist: The Making of The Beatles’ “A Day in the Life”

title: Where Did the Monk’s Haircut Come From? A Look at the Rich and Contentious History of the Tonsure
description:
in History, Religion |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

One might assume from a mod­ern view­point that the hair­styles worn by monks a
body:
in History, Religion |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

One might assume from a mod­ern view­point that the hair­styles worn by monks arose to deal with male pat­tern bald­ness anx­i­ety. As in the school uni­form approach, you can’t sin­gle out one person’s bald­ness when every­one is bald. But this, again, would be a mod­ern view, full of the van­i­ty the tonsured—those with reli­gious­ly shaven heads—ostensibly vowed to renounce. Accord­ing to the Catholic Ency­clo­pe­dia, the ton­sure (from the Latin verb for “to shear”) began as a “badge of slav­ery” among Greeks and Romans. It was adopt­ed “on this very account” by ear­ly monas­tic orders, to mark the total sur­ren­der of the will.
Would it sur­prise you, then, to learn that there were ton­sure wars? Prob­a­bly not if you know any­thing about church his­to­ry. Every arti­cle of cloth­ing and of faith has sparked some major con­tro­ver­sy at one time or anoth­er. So too with the ton­sure, of which—we learn in the Vox video above—there were three styles. The first, the coro­nal (or Roman or Petrine) ton­sure, is the one we see in count­less Medieval and Renais­sance paint­ings: a bald pate at the crown sur­round­ed by a fringe of hair, pos­si­bly meant to evoke the crown of thorns. Next is the Pauline, a ful­ly shaved head, seen much less in West­ern art since it was “used more com­mon­ly in East­ern Ortho­doxy.”
The third style of ton­sure caused all the trou­ble. Or rather, it was this style that served as a vis­i­ble sign of reli­gious dif­fer­ences between the Roman Catholic Church and the church­es in Britain and Ire­land. “Celtic Catholi­cism was ‘out of sync’ with the Roman Catholic Church,” notes Vox. “Roman Catholics would use the dif­fer­ences between them to por­tray Celtic Catholi­cism as pagan, or even as an off­shoot, cel­e­brat­ing the pow­er-hun­gry magi­cian, Simon Magus.” The Celtic ton­sure fell under a cloud, but how exact­ly did it dif­fer from the oth­ers? Since it dis­ap­peared in the ear­ly Mid­dle Ages and few images seem to have sur­vived, no one seems sure.
Daniel McCarthy, fel­low emer­i­tus at Trin­i­ty Col­lege, Dublin set out to solve the mys­tery. He spec­u­lates that the Celtic ton­sure, as you’ll see on a com­put­er-sim­u­lat­ed monk’s head, was a tri­an­gu­lar shape, with the apex at the front. When the Roman Catholics took over Ire­land, all of the vest­ments, dates, and hair­cuts were slow­ly brought into line with the dom­i­nant view. The prac­tice of ton­sure offi­cial­ly end­ed in 1972, and fell out of favor in Eng­lish-speak­ing coun­tries cen­turies ear­li­er, accord­ing to the Catholic Ency­clo­pe­dia. But in any case, McCarthy sees the ton­sure not as a spurn­ing of fash­ion, but as a cult-like devo­tion to style. In that sense, we can see peo­ple who adopt sim­i­lar hair­cuts around the world as still visu­al­ly sig­nal­ing their mem­ber­ship in some kind of order, reli­gious or oth­er­wise.
Note: An ear­li­er ver­sion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
Relat­ed Con­tent:
How a Bal­ti­more Hair­dress­er Became a World-Renowned “Hair Archae­ol­o­gist” of Ancient Rome
Ani­mat­ed: Stephen Fry & Ann Wid­de­combe Debate the Catholic Church
50 Years of Chang­ing David Bowie Hair Styles in One Ani­mat­ed GIF
Josh Jones is a writer and musi­cian based in Durham, NC.

schema.headline: Where Did the Monk’s Haircut Come From? A Look at the Rich and Contentious History of the Tonsure

title: A High-Definition Cinematic Video Relives NASA's Artemis II Mission
description: Between April 1 and 10 of this year, NASA’s Artemis II mission made history as four astronauts flew farther from Earth than anyone has ever travel
body:
Between April 1 and 10 of this year, NASA’s Artemis II mission made history as four astronauts flew farther from Earth than anyone has ever traveled before. With state-of-the-art technology and numerous cameras onboard, the crew captured some of the most striking images we’ve seen of our planet as the flight path led them around our iconic satellite. Lenses attached to the exterior of the spacecraft, plus numerous photographers stationed on the ground during the launch, documented the literally out-of-the-this world process of rocketing through the planet’s atmosphere and keeping course around the moon.
John D. Boswell, a musician and filmmaker who works as MELODYSHEEP, created a cinematic short film compiled from videos and images published by NASA. His original music accompanies the public domain images and soundbites, providing a uniquely immersive glimpse of the historic mission. See more of his work on his Vimeo and YouTube channels.

schema.headline: A High-Definition Cinematic Video Relives NASA's Artemis II Mission

title: Presence, Memory, and Time Merge in the Surreal Works of 'Architects of Absence'
description: “Nothingness lies coiled in the heart of being,” philosopher and writer Jean-Paul Sartre posited in Being and Nothingness, published in 1943. It’s
body:
“Nothingness lies coiled in the heart of being,” philosopher and writer Jean-Paul Sartre posited in Being and Nothingness, published in 1943. It’s in this book that he also shares an anecdote about stepping into a cafe, where he was to meet a friend. In scanning the room with its other patrons, furniture, and noise, he was tuned into the fact that his friend was not there, an experience infused with an acute awareness—an absence that transformed into a kind of presence.
Architects of Absence, a group exhibition at Gallery Wendi Norris, plumbs this dialogue between two seemingly contradictory elements. Colossal readers may recognize the work of Dabin Ahn, Selva Aparicio, and Remedios Varo (1908-1963) alongside Marcel Jean (1900-1993) and Yaxkin Fuentes-Barrientos. The latter is currently artist-in-residence at the Frank Lloyd Wright-designed Martin House in Buffalo, New York, where she sources disused clay roof tiles on which she then carved fallen leaves.
Dabin Ahn, “Nocturne IV” (2026), oil on linen over panel, jasper, brass, adhesive, camphor, and sapele in walnut artist’s frame, 13 x 11 inches
Through surreal compositions in a variety of media, including Fuentes-Barrientos’ architectural graphite drawings and oil paintings and Ahn’s dreamy meditations on loss and the passage of time, viewers are invited to reflect upon the nature of not only presence in a physical sense but also the emotional and psychological sense of being present. Silhouettes, cutouts, buildings and sky, and light and shadow assemble into symbolic motifs of remembrance and visibility.
The gallery says, “Whether investigating the psychic, mythic, and alchemical realms of the imagination, documenting the material remnants of industrial and biological change, or harnessing loss and memory into resonant forms, their works map the absences that define reality.
Architects of Absence continues through August 29 in San Francisco. 
Yaxkin Fuentes-Barrientos, “Dormant Pulpits” (2026), oil and pastel on linen, 24 x 30 inches
Dabin Ahn, “Torso (Full Moon)” (2026), oil on linen over panel, mother of pearl inlay, cast resin, and hardware, 32 x 12 inches
Selva Aparicio, “Roof Traces II” (2026), carved clay roof tiles, courtesy of Frank Lloyd Wright’s Martin House, Buffalo, New York, 12 x 6 x 1/2 inches
Marcel Jean, “Conductibilité des roches mères” (1974), gouache and flottage on Masonite, 7 1/4 x 8 5/8 inches
Dabin Ahn, “Golden Days (Animated)” (2026), oil on linen over panel, plexiglass, single-board computer, LCD display with video, cables, power supply, hardware, and walnut, 15 3/4 x 9 1/2 inches
Yaxkin Fuentes-Barrientos, “Spirit of the Beehive” (2026), ink on found paper, 10 x 13 1/2 inches (framed)

schema.headline: Presence, Memory, and Time Merge in the Surreal Works of 'Architects of Absence'

title: A Homerist Reacts to Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey”
description:
Film Review
                “This is a tale of a man affected by trauma, but without any sense that it changed him at all.”
body:
Film Review
                “This is a tale of a man affected by trauma, but without any sense that it changed him at all.”
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Director Christopher Nolan on the set of The Odyssey (2026) (all images courtesy Universal)
    
        
            This is not Homer’s Odyssey. Let’s get that out of the way. As a Homerist, I had to remind myself of this throughout a film that reduces a narrative of 12,000 lines to just under three hours of airtime. The cuts are significant for someone who knows Homer well: The entire Phaeacian episode is gone; Matt Damon’s Odysseus never meets his father. Where the Homeric epic splits and gives its hero 12 books on Ithaca, the movie is about two-thirds journey and flashback with under an hour once Odysseus returns home.The middle of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey (2026) delivers visceral discomfort. When the witch Circe (Samantha Morton) serves her transformative feast to Odysseus’s companions on the island of Aeaea, the camera tracks their voracious hunger, following their shift from greedily slurping, increasingly frantic men into squealing pigs. Circe physically wrests them out of one shape into another, later commenting that their appetites made them what they truly are.This scene bears the promise of what the magic of modern film can do for ancient myth. It is physically affective, making vivid what could only be imagined before. And it also allows for the adaptation of the story: This Circe has been abused by travelers before. The animals who lurk on her island are the men who have met their comeuppance.
                  
                      
                        Film Review
                      
                      
                        Film
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        The Odyssey

schema.headline: A Homerist Reacts to Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey”

title: The Sunflower Effect of Kyung-Me
description:
Art Review
								The artist’s current series marks a shift in her practice, evoking the life span of a sunflower — from blooming to dyin
body:
Art Review
								The artist’s current series marks a shift in her practice, evoking the life span of a sunflower — from blooming to dying, from sharply defined forms to rivulets of dissolution.
						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Kyung-Me, "Untitled" (2024), ink and watercolor on mulberry paper (all images courtesy the artist and Bureau, New York)
    
		
				
						Kyung-Me has yet to be recognized as one of the most compelling artists of her generation. Now in her mid-30s, she has transformed her process to make at least four distinct bodies of work. First, she published her graphic novel, Bad Korean (2016), a pictorial diary done in pastel and colored pencil of a gullible young woman in New York, looking for love. Alienated and unhappy, she believes sex, alcohol, food, and dressing hip will help her escape her hyper-awareness of not fitting in. Told in snippets of speech, such as the repeated phrase “I want to be your only friend,” the self-deprecating narrative is an engrossing combination of quiet desperation and blunt humor. It is easy to imagine that Kyung-Me could have authored a series of books about this character, but she didn’t. In Copy Kitty (2020), Kyung-Me used graphite to depict an intricate, wordless tale of a cat who keeps changing her identity in the pursuit of love and affection from a young blonde man.While the themes of alienation and trying to fit in — a common trope for those living in the diaspora — convey a narrative ripe for exploration, Kyung-Me shifted everything about her work for her first solo exhibition in 2022 at Bureau, which I reviewed. Working meticulously with pen and pencil, she created eight drawings of elaborately detailed, nested rooms. About this dream house, whose occupant’s face is always hidden, I wrote that it was "as if she drew each line with a razor blade that cuts deep but draws no blood. The effect is mesmerizing and unsettling."
									
											
												Art Review
											
											
												Review
											
											
												New York

title: New Museum's Shiny New Building Leaks During Flash Floods
description:
News
                No artworks were harmed, but extreme weather shuttered the Lower Manhattan museum for a full weekend.
body:
News
                No artworks were harmed, but extreme weather shuttered the Lower Manhattan museum for a full weekend. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            A view of the new New Museum on the Bowery in Manhattan (photo Hakim Bishara/Hyperallergic)
    
        
            Just a few months after the reopening of the New Museum's expanded building, a water leak shut down the Manhattan institution for a full weekend. According to Artnews, museum workers scrambled to address a "significant" leak during the flash floods of July 18, using buckets to catch water that streamed into a gallery housing artist WangShui's piece "oppose the serpent" (2024). In an email to Hyperallergic, the New Museum said that "immediate steps were taken to address the leak," and that no artwork was harmed. In an Instagram post on Saturday, the museum announced that it would be closing for the weekend due to "extreme weather in New York City."Fourth-four gallery installation view of New Humans: Memories of the Future (photo Valentina Di Liscia/Hyperallergic)The Lower Manhattan museum unveiled its $82 million OMA-designed expansion in March after two years of closure. The 60,000-square-foot expansion doubled the institution’s exhibition space, adding much-needed breathing space, but as Aaron Short reported for Hyperallergic, "bits of masking tape were still stuck to the stairway railings, and a handful of missing wall panels revealed internal wires, rebar, and insulation."WangShui's work is included in the inaugural exhibition New Humans: Memories of the Future, which explores what it means to be human amid the cataclysmic technological changes of the 20th and 21st centuries through the work of more than 150 artists.  The new building received mixed reviews from Hyperallergic's critics with Editor-at-Large Hrag Vartanian calling it a "mishmash of Obama-era startup culture, dated by the lime green and perforated metal." 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New Museum
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: New Museum's Shiny New Building Leaks During Flash Floods
